Boolean Functions
Introduction
?

We can associate any logical operation (or expression) with a corresponding Boolean function. In this correspondence, the value is equivalent to "True," and the value is equivalent to "False."

For example, consider the logical operation of conjunction (the "and" connective). The corresponding truth table is shown below. Remember, conjunction is true if and only if both arguments are true.

Now, substituting for "False" and for "True" in the table, we get the following:

The obtained table defines the Boolean function associated with the logical operation of conjunction.

Peirce Arrow, Sheffer Stroke, and XOR
?

Remember that we equate to "False" and to "True." As well as conjunction, disjunction, negation, implication, and equivalence, there are a few more logical operations we should know:

  • The exclusive disjunction (EXCLUSIVE OR or XOR) is false if and only if both and are false or both and are true The corresponding Boolean function is the following:

    The key difference between XOR and is that XOR returns zero when Hence, it is called the exclusive OR since it returns if or equal but not both.

  • The Peirce arrow (NOT OR or NOR) is true if and only if both and are false The corresponding Boolean function is the following:

    Note that the Peirce arrow is the negation of the Boolean OR operator:

  • The Sheffer stroke (NOT AND or NAND) is false if and only if both and are true The corresponding Boolean function is the following:
    The Sheffer stroke is the negation of the Boolean AND operator:

Classifying Unary Logical Operations
?

There are distinct Boolean functions of variables; therefore, there are distinct Boolean functions of one variable. These functions can be summarized in the following table:

Each of these functions corresponds to an unary logical operation, an operation that operates on only one operand. Let's examine each function in turn.

  • The function always returns This is the constant zero operation

  • The function returns the same value as This is the the identity

  • The function returns the opposite value to This is the negation

  • The function always returns This is the constant one operation

Classifying Binary Logical Operations
?

If we have two variables, the situation is a bit more complicated. There are distinct Boolean functions of two variables. These functions can be summarized in the following table:

Each of these functions corresponds to a binary logical function, an operation that operates on two operands. Let's examine each function in turn.

  • The following functions involve conjunction, disjunction, negation, implication, and equivalence.

    • The function always returns This is the constant zero operation

    • The function returns if and only if both and equal This is the conjunction

    • The function returns the same value as This is the identity applied to the first input

    • The function returns the same value as This is the identity applied to the second input

    • The function returns if and only if both and equal This is the disjunction

    • The function returns if and only if both This is the equivalence

    • The function returns the value opposite to This is the negation of the second input

    • The function returns if and only if and This is the implication

    • The function returns the value opposite to This is the negation of the first input

    • The function returns if and only if and This is the implication

    • The function always returns This is the constant one operation

  • The following involve exclusive disjunction, Peirce arrow, and Sheffer Stroke.

    • The function returns if and only if both and are or both are This is the exclusive disjunction Notice that it's opposite to the function hence,

    • The function returns if and only if both and equal This is the Peirce arrow Notice that it's opposite to the function hence,

    • The function returns if and only if both and equal This is the Sheffer stroke Notice that it's opposite to the function hence,

  • Finally, we have two other functions.

    • The function returns if and only if and Notice that it's opposite to the function Hence,

    • The function returns if and only if and Notice that it's opposite to the function Hence,
